12 min read
Just now

--

A multi-agent decision system in LangGraph and Snowflake Cortex, and why not every part of it should be an LLM.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Mathurin NAPOLY / matnapo on UnsplashWatch a medical helicopter drop onto a highway crash site and it looks like a single act: the aircraft was there when it was needed. It isn’t a single act. Somewhere, a dispatcher took the call and decided this was a flight. Upstream of that, someone had a weather read good enough to say go. Further upstream, the right aircraft had to be mechanically airworthy and staged at the right base, with a rested, qualified crew on it. Any one of those falls through and the helicopter doesn’t come.
Now run that across a whole region (a dozen aircraft, several bases, crews on rotation, weather moving through, demand that spikes without warning) and the picture the coordination depends on is scattered across systems that don’t talk to each other. Which aircraft are down for maintenance sits in one place. What the weather’s doing across the bases is somewhere else. Whether there’s enough rested, qualified crew to fly is a third. Pulling all of it into a single read is left to whoever’s making the call.
A lot of that reconciliation ends up happening in someone’s head. Experienced people are often good at it, but it doesn’t scale past the…
